title: Potato salad
subtitle: Waxy new potatoes in a mustardy mayonnaise with chives.
excerpt: Waxy new potatoes boiled until just tender, then dressed warm in a mixture of mayonnaise, crème fraîche, Dijon mustard, and chopped chives. Dressing the potatoes warm lets them absorb the flavour rather than sitting in it.
sourceNotes: Potato salad appears in British cookery books from at least the nineteenth century, when cold dressed potatoes were served alongside cold meats at supper. The mustardy mayonnaise version became the standard British form through the twentieth century. The technique of dressing the potatoes warm to aid absorption appears in Constance Spry and Hume's The Constance Spry Cookery Book (1956) and is the reliable version.

content.text: Potato salad made with warm potatoes is not the same thing as potato salad made with cold ones. Warm potatoes are porous: they soak up the dressing as they cool, so every piece is flavoured through. Cold potatoes sit in the dressing but do not absorb it.

Use waxy potatoes throughout, Jersey Royals in early summer, small Charlotte or Anya potatoes the rest of the year. Floury varieties break up when boiled and do not hold their shape when tossed in dressing.
A medium saucepan and a large mixing bowl for tossing and serving. No other kit needed.

Place the potatoes in a medium saucepan. Cover with cold water and add {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt. Bring to the boil, then simmer for 15 to 18 minutes until just tender when pierced with a knife. Drain and leave to steam for 2 minutes.

While the potatoes cook, mix {{mayonnaise}} of mayonnaise, {{creme-fraiche}} of crème fraîche, {{dijon-mustard}} of Dijon mustard, and {{cider-vinegar}} of cider vinegar together in a large bowl. Season with {{black-pepper}} of black pepper.

While the potatoes are still warm, tip them into the bowl with the dressing. Halve any larger ones. Toss gently to coat. Leave at room temperature for 20 minutes to absorb the dressing, then scatter over the chives and serve. Or refrigerate for up to two hours before serving.

items.fix: Use waxy potatoes only, Charlotte, Anya, or Jersey Royals. Check for tenderness at 15 minutes and stop cooking as soon as a knife slides in easily.
items.cause: Overcooked, or a floury variety was used instead of waxy.

items.fix: Add a little more mayonnaise and crème fraîche. This is not a problem with the recipe. It means the potatoes did their job.
items.cause: Potatoes were dressed when very hot, absorbing too much dressing.
items.symptom: Dressing has soaked in and the salad looks dry

items.fix: Add another teaspoon of cider vinegar and taste. The dressing should have a distinct tang.
Drain and dress immediately after boiling. Refrigerate promptly if not serving within an hour.
items.cause: Left in water for too long after boiling, or made too far in advance without refrigerating.

content.text: Potato salad is a picnic and barbecue constant across Britain, Germany, the United States, and most of northern Europe. The British version favours mayonnaise and mustard where the German version leans on vinegar and the American one adds celery and sweet pickle. The recipe here occupies the British register: creamy, mustardy, and finished with chives rather than dill. It goes with cold meats, smoked fish, and most things cooked over a fire.
